name: Sliding Window
concept:
## The idea

A **sliding window** is two pointers (`left`, `right`) that bound a *contiguous* range. You **grow** the window by moving `right`, and when some condition is violated, you **shrink** it by moving `left`. Each element enters once and leaves once → **O(n)**.

It replaces the brute-force 'check every subarray/substring' (O(n²) or worse) whenever the thing you're tracking can be updated incrementally as the window moves.

Two flavors:
- **Variable-size window** — grow/shrink to maintain a constraint (e.g. 'no repeated characters', 'sum ≥ target'). This is the common interview flavor.
- **Fixed-size window** — window of exactly `k`; slide it one step at a time.

## Python you'll pick up here

- `set` / `dict` to track window contents (ties directly into Hashing)
- `while` loop to shrink: `while <window invalid>: ... left += 1`
- `float('inf')` as a starting 'infinitely large' answer for minimization

Sliding window = Two Pointers + Hashing combined, so you've already built the pieces.
whenToUse: The problem asks about a contiguous subarray or substring
You want the longest/shortest/〈best〉 window satisfying some property
A brute force would re-examine overlapping ranges repeatedly
You can update the answer incrementally as elements enter/leave the window

problems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
problems.keyIdeas: Grow the window by moving `right`. Keep a `set` of characters currently inside. When you hit a character already in the window, **shrink from the left** until the duplicate is gone. Track the largest window size seen. Every character is added and removed at most once → O(n).
hints: Think of the answer as a window with no repeats. What do you do when adding a character would create a repeat?
Keep a set of characters in the current window. If the new character is already there, remove from the left until it's not.
After each step, update your best length with (right - left + 1).

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Check every possible substring to see if it has all unique characters. For each starting position i, expand to j and track the longest valid substring. Motivates why we need a window approach.

solutions.label: Sliding window with a set (Sumanth's solution)
solutions.keyIdea: When the new char is a repeat, advance left past the earlier occurrence before adding it.
solutions.code:
def lengthOfLongestSubstring(s: str) -> int:
    charSet = set()
    i,j  = 0,0
    n = len(s)
    if n==0: return 0
    maxLen = 1
    while(j<n):
        if s[j] in charSet:
            while s[i]!=s[j]:
                charSet.remove(s[i])
                i += 1
            i+= 1
        charSet.add(s[j])
        maxLen = max(maxLen,j-i+1)
        j += 1
    return maxLen

solutions.label: Optimal — window with last-seen index map
solutions.keyIdea: Remember each character's last index; on a repeat, jump `left` directly past it instead of shrinking one step at a time. Slightly faster constant factor than the set approach, but same O(n). Use when you want to avoid the inner while loop.

problems.description: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without duplicate characters.

problems.title: Minimum Size Subarray Sum
problems.keyIdeas: Grow the window (add `nums[right]` to a running sum). **Whenever the sum is ≥ target, try to shrink** from the left to find the smallest valid window, updating the best length. Works because all numbers are positive, so shrinking only ever decreases the sum. O(n).
hints: Keep a running sum of the current window instead of recomputing it.
Once the running sum reaches target, the window is valid — but maybe too big. Can you shrink from the left while it stays valid?
Track the minimum window length over all valid windows; return 0 if you never reach target.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Check every subarray by computing its sum from scratch. For each starting position i, try all ending positions j ≥ i. Track the smallest valid subarray. Motivates why incremental sum tracking is critical.

solutions.label: Grow-then-shrink window (Sumanth's solution)
solutions.keyIdea: Expand right; while the sum qualifies, contract left and record the smallest length.

problems.description: Given an array of positive integers nums and a positive integer target, return the minimal length of a subarray whose sum is greater than or equal to target. If there is no such subarray, return 0 instead.

problems.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

video.title: NeetCode — Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock (Python)

problems.keyIdeas: Track the minimum price seen so far as you scan left-to-right. At each day, compute the profit if you sold today (today's price - min so far) and update the best profit. This is a degenerate sliding window — the 'buy day' is implicitly `left`, 'sell day' is `right`. O(n) time, O(1) space.
hints: You can only sell after you buy. What's the best you can do if you sold on day i?
Keep track of the lowest price you've seen before today. The profit on day i is price[i] - min_so_far.
Scan once, updating the minimum and the best profit as you go.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Check every pair (buy day i, sell day j where j > i) and track the max profit.

solutions.label: Optimal
solutions.keyIdea: Track the minimum price seen so far; at each step, compute profit if we sold today.
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def maxProfit(self, prices: List[int]) -> int:
        min_price = float('inf')
        max_profit = 0
        for price in prices:
            # If we bought at min_price and sold today
            profit = price - min_price
            max_profit = max(max_profit, profit)
            # Update the cheapest buy opportunity so far
            min_price = min(min_price, price)
        return max_profit

problems.description: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the ith day.

problems.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
problems.keyIdeas: Grow the window. Track character counts in a dict. The **most frequent character** in the window is what you'd keep; the rest you'd replace. If `(window size - max frequency) > k`, you've exceeded k replacements — shrink from the left. The answer is the largest valid window size. O(n) time.
hints: In any valid window, you pick the most common character and replace all others. How many replacements does that take?
If the window has length L and the most frequent char appears M times, you need (L - M) replacements. This must be ≤ k.
Use a dict to count characters in the window. Grow right; when (window_size - max_count) > k, shrink left.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Check every substring. For each, count characters and see if (length - max_frequency) ≤ k. Straightforward but O(n²). Shows why the window approach is essential.
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def characterReplacement(self, s: str, k: int) -> int:
        n = len(s)
        max_len = 0
        # Try every starting position
        for i in range(n):
            count = {}  # char -> frequency in current substring
            max_freq = 0
            # Try every ending position from i
            for j in range(i, n):
                count[s[j]] = count.get(s[j], 0) + 1
                max_freq = max(max_freq, count[s[j]])
                window_len = j - i + 1
                # Check if this substring is valid
                if window_len - max_freq <= k:
                    max_len = max(max_len, window_len)
        return max_len

solutions.label: Optimal — sliding window with max frequency
solutions.keyIdea: Expand the window; track character counts. When (window size - max frequency) > k, shrink left. The key insight: we keep the most common char and replace all others. Each element enters and exits once, giving O(n).
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def characterReplacement(self, s: str, k: int) -> int:
        count = {}  # char -> frequency in current window
        left = 0
        max_len = 0
        max_freq = 0  # highest frequency of any single char in the window
        
        for right in range(len(s)):
            count[s[right]] = count.get(s[right], 0) + 1
            max_freq = max(max_freq, count[s[right]])
            
            # Number of chars we'd need to replace = window_size - max_freq
            window_size = right - left + 1
            if window_size - max_freq > k:
                # Window invalid, shrink from left
                count[s[left]] -= 1
                left += 1
            
            max_len = max(max_len, right - left + 1)
        
        return max_len

problems.description: You are given a string s and an integer k. You can choose any character of the string and change it to any other uppercase English character. You can perform this operation at most k times.

problems.title: Permutation in String
problems.keyIdeas: A permutation means the same character counts. Use a **fixed-size window** of length `len(s1)` sliding over `s2`. Track character counts in the window; when they match `s1`'s counts, you found it. Use two dicts and compare, or count matches. O(n) with 26-letter alphabet.
hints: Two strings are permutations if they have the same character counts.
Slide a window of size len(s1) across s2. For each window position, check if the character counts match s1.
You can use two Counter dicts and compare them, or track the number of 'matching character counts' and check if it reaches 26.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: For every substring of s2 with length len(s1), check if it's a permutation of s1 by comparing sorted versions or character counts. O(n·m log m) where n = len(s2), m = len(s1).

solutions.label: Better — fixed window with count match
solutions.keyIdea: Maintain a window of size len(s1). Track character counts; when counts match s1, return True. Slide the window one step at a time. O(n) because we update counts incrementally instead of recomputing.

solutions.label: Optimal — match counter
solutions.keyIdea: Track how many of the 26 letters have the correct count in the window. When matches == 26, return True. Avoids the O(26) dict comparison on every slide by maintaining a match count. Best constant factor.

problems.description: Given two strings s1 and s2, return true if s2 contains a permutation of s1, or false otherwise.

problems.title: arithmetic Slices
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: TODO: Add key ideas
solutions.code:
nums = [1,2,3,5,6,7,8]


def arithmeticSlices(nums):
    runningDifference = 0
    i,j, totalSlices = 0, 0, 0

    while j+1<len(nums):
        j += 1
        lastDifference = nums[j] - nums[j-1]
        if not lastDifference==runningDifference:
            i = j-1
            runningDifference = lastDifference
        elif j-i>=2:
            totalSlices += (j-i-1)
    return totalSlices
print(arithmeticSlices(nums))

problems.description: An integer array is called arithmetic if it consists of at least three elements and if the difference between any two consecutive elements is the same.

problems.title: count Subarrays With Score Lessthan K
problems.keyIdeas: #increment i if score is more tham or equal to k         #increment j if you can
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: #increment i if score is more tham or equal to k         #increment j if you can
solutions.code:
def getNumSubArrays(i, j, prevJ):
    if(prevJ<i):
        return ((j-i+1)*(j-i+2))/2
    else:
        return ((j-i+1)*(j-i+2))/2 - getNumSubArrays(i, prevJ, -1)


def countSubArrysWithScoreLessThanK(arr, k):
    numSubArrays = 0
    i = 0
    j = 0
    n = len(arr)
    prevJ = -1
    sumFromIToJ = arr[0]
    while(j<n):
        #increment i if score is more tham or equal to k
        print("istart: ",i, j, prevJ, sumFromIToJ)
        while(sumFromIToJ*(j-i+1)>=k):
            sumFromIToJ = sumFromIToJ - arr[i]
            i = i+1
            if(i>j and i<n):
                j = i
                sumFromIToJ = sumFromIToJ + arr[j]
            if(i==n):
                break

        print("inc i: ",i, j, prevJ, sumFromIToJ)
        #increment j if you can
        while(j+1<n and (sumFromIToJ+arr[j+1])*(j-i+2)<k):
            j = j+1
            sumFromIToJ = sumFromIToJ + arr[j]
            
        print("inc j: ",i, j, prevJ, sumFromIToJ)
        numSubArrays = numSubArrays + getNumSubArrays(i, j, prevJ)
        
        prevJ = j
        i += 1
        j += 1
        if j<n:
            sumFromIToJ = sumFromIToJ-arr[i-1] + arr[j]
        print("inc i,j: ",i, j, prevJ, sumFromIToJ)
        print()

    return numSubArrays

nums = [1,2,1]
k = 6
print(countSubArrysWithScoreLessThanK(nums, k))
        

        
'''
def countSubarrays(self, nums: List[int], k: int) -> int:
    sum, res, j = 0, 0, 0
    for i, n in enumerate(nums):
        sum += n
        while sum * (i - j + 1) >= k:
            sum -= nums[j]
            j += 1
        res += i - j + 1
    return res
    
    
    adds all the subarrays that ends at i starting at j or later, both inclusive that are valid
    
    
        
https://leetcode.com/problems/count-subarrays-with-score-less-than-k/discuss/2138778/Sliding-Window
        
        '''

problems.title: maximize The Confusion Of An Exam
problems.keyIdeas: # only False to True             # only move left till both are wrong
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: # only False to True             # only move left till both are wrong
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def maxConsecutiveAnswers(self, answerKey: str, k: int) -> int:
        # only False to True
        countOfFalsesChangesToTrue, maxConsecutiveTruesSeen = 0, 0
        right = left = 0
        while right<len(answerKey):
            if answerKey[right]=="F": countOfFalsesChangesToTrue += 1
            while countOfFalsesChangesToTrue>k:
                if answerKey[left]=="F": countOfFalsesChangesToTrue -= 1
                left += 1
            right += 1
            maxConsecutiveTruesSeen = max(maxConsecutiveTruesSeen, right-left)
        return maxConsecutiveTruesSeen
    


class Solution:
    def maxConsecutiveAnswers(self, answerKey: str, k: int) -> int:

        countOfFalsesChangesToTrue = countOfTruesChangesToTrue = maxConsecutiveSeen = 0
        right = leftOfTrues = leftOfFalses = 0

        while right<len(answerKey):
            if answerKey[right]=="F": countOfFalsesChangesToTrue += 1
            while countOfFalsesChangesToTrue>k:
                if answerKey[leftOfTrues]=="F": countOfFalsesChangesToTrue -= 1
                leftOfTrues += 1
            
            if answerKey[right]=="T": countOfTruesChangesToTrue += 1
            while countOfTruesChangesToTrue>k:
                if answerKey[leftOfFalses]=="T": countOfTruesChangesToTrue -= 1
                leftOfFalses += 1

            right += 1
            maxConsecutiveSeen = max(maxConsecutiveSeen, right-min(leftOfTrues, leftOfFalses))

        return maxConsecutiveSeen
    

    
class Solution:
    def maxConsecutiveAnswers(self, answerKey: str, k: int) -> int:

        countOfFalsesChangesToTrue = countOfTruesChangesToTrue = maxConsecutiveSeen = 0
        right = left = 0

        while right<len(answerKey):
            
            if answerKey[right]=="F": countOfFalsesChangesToTrue += 1
            else: countOfTruesChangesToTrue += 1

            # only move left till both are wrong
            while countOfFalsesChangesToTrue>k and countOfTruesChangesToTrue>k:
                if answerKey[left]=="F": countOfFalsesChangesToTrue -= 1
                else: countOfTruesChangesToTrue -= 1
                left += 1

            right += 1
            maxConsecutiveSeen = max(maxConsecutiveSeen, right-left)

        return maxConsecutiveSeen

problems.description: A teacher is writing a test with n true/false questions, with 'T' denoting true and 'F' denoting false. He wants to confuse the students by maximizing the number of consecutive questions with the same answer (multiple trues or multiple falses in a row).

problems.description: You are given a string s and an array of strings words. All the strings of words are of the same length.

problems.title: Minimum Window Substring
problems.keyIdeas: Expand `right` to include characters from `t`. Once the window **contains all characters of `t`** (tracked with counts), try shrinking from `left` to find the smallest valid window. Track the best (smallest) valid window seen. Use two dicts: one for `t`'s requirements, one for the current window. O(n + m) time.
hints: Use a dict to count required characters from t. Grow the window until it satisfies all requirements.
Once the window is valid (contains all chars of t with correct counts), try shrinking from the left to minimize it.
Track how many required characters are satisfied. When that count equals the number of unique chars in t, the window is valid.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Try every substring of s. For each, check if it contains all characters of t with correct counts. Track the smallest valid substring. O(n²·m) where n = len(s), m = len(t).

solutions.label: Optimal — sliding window with need/have tracking
solutions.keyIdea: Expand to cover all of t, then shrink to minimize. Track 'have' vs 'need' for each character. Use a 'formed' counter to know when the window is valid in O(1). Each element enters and exits once, giving O(n + m).

problems.description: Given two strings s and t of lengths m and n respectively, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window. If there is no such substring, return the empty string "".

problems.title: Sliding Window Maximum
problems.keyIdeas: Use a **deque** (double-ended queue) to track indices of elements in **decreasing order of value**. When adding a new element, remove smaller elements from the back (they can never be the max). Remove indices outside the window from the front. The front of the deque is always the current max. O(n) — each element is added and removed once.
hints: A naive approach would recompute the max for each window position (O(n·k)). Can you maintain the max incrementally?
Use a deque to store indices. Keep it sorted by value (largest at the front). When the window slides, remove indices that fall out of range.
Before adding a new element, pop all smaller elements from the back of the deque — they'll never be the answer while this element is in the window.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: For each window position, compute the max of the k elements in O(k).

solutions.label: Optimal — monotonic deque
solutions.keyIdea: Maintain a deque of indices in decreasing order of value. The front is the current max.

problems.description: You are given an array of integers nums, there is a sliding window of size k which is moving from the very left of the array to the very right. You can only see the k numbers in the window. Each time the sliding window moves right by one position.
